cases.title: Lung lobectomy: invasive adenocarcinoma, acinar predominant
cases.specimenType: Right upper lobectomy with hilar lymph nodes
cases.clinicalHistory: 67-year-old former smoker with a 2.4 cm spiculated right upper lobe nodule and no radiographic nodal disease.

image.caption: Invasive lung adenocarcinoma with acinar growth pattern.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: chest radiograph and clinicopathologic details of the lesions a upper panel chest computed tomography scan l1 l2 and l3 were in the right upper lobe rul right middle lobe rml and right lower lobe rll respectively l4 l5 and l6 were all observed in the left upper lobe lul no hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy was observed bottom panel the corresponding positron emission tomography pet ct scans revealed intense 18f fluorodeoxyglucose fdg avidity b tumor lesion images c microscopic images of hematoxylin and eosin stained sections representing the predominant histologic subtypes two representative areas are shown in a and b the corresponding histologic subtypes are described in table 1

diagnosticSteps: Classify the tumor as invasive adenocarcinoma and estimate predominant and secondary histologic patterns.
Measure total tumor size and invasive size, then assess visceral pleural invasion, lymphovascular invasion, spread through air spaces, and margins.
Evaluate all submitted hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes and assign pathologic stage.
Ensure molecular testing triage is documented for a resected nonsquamous non-small cell carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: TTF-1 and Napsin A
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma when morphology or history is uncertain.

ancillaryOptions.name: Broad molecular profiling with EGFR and ALK coverage
ancillaryOptions.result: Recommended for therapeutic biomarker assessment in appropriate clinical context.

ancillaryOptions.name: p40
ancillaryOptions.result: Not required for classic adenocarcinoma but useful if squamous differentiation is suspected.

cases.reportingTarget: Lung, right upper lobe, lobectomy: Invasive adenocarcinoma, acinar predominant, 2.4 cm total and invasive size, margins negative, no visceral pleural invasion, 0 of 6 lymph nodes involved, pT1c pN0.
pitfalls: Do not use total lepidic-containing size as invasive size when staging part-solid adenocarcinoma.
Do not omit visceral pleural elastin assessment if pleural relationship is equivocal.
Do not forget biomarker triage for resected nonsquamous carcinoma.

cases.title: Endobronchial biopsy: squamous cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 70-year-old current smoker with hemoptysis and a centrally obstructing left mainstem bronchial mass.

image.caption: Squamous cell carcinoma of lung with keratinizing nests.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: flexible bronchoscopy immediately following re intubation revealed a tumour obstructing the left mainstem bronchus lmsb with ball valve effect a and residual tumour and clot at the right mainstem bronchus rmsb b view from main carina after urgent flexible bronchoscopy with extraction of lmsb endobronchial tumour embolism c minimal residual tumour at rmsb d

diagnosticSteps: Identify invasive malignant nests with squamous differentiation, keratinization, or intercellular bridges.
Use a tissue-sparing immunopanel only if morphology is poorly differentiated or crush-limited.
Exclude metastatic squamous carcinoma from head and neck or skin using clinical context and targeted stains when needed.
Preserve tissue for PD-L1 or additional biomarkers if clinically requested.

ancillaryOptions.name: p40 and TTF-1
ancillaryOptions.result: p40 positive and TTF-1 negative supports squamous cell carcinoma over adenocarcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: PD-L1 immunohistochemistry
ancillaryOptions.result: Provides predictive biomarker information for advanced non-small cell carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: Mucicarmine
ancillaryOptions.result: Reserve for suspected adenocarcinoma if glandular differentiation is unclear.

cases.reportingTarget: Lung, left mainstem bronchus, biopsy: Squamous cell carcinoma, keratinizing type.
pitfalls: Do not exhaust scant biopsy tissue with broad immunopanels when morphology is diagnostic.
Do not call primary lung squamous carcinoma without considering metastatic disease in the right clinical setting.
Do not ignore crush artifact that may obscure small cell carcinoma.

cases.title: Mediastinal node biopsy: small cell carcinoma
cases.specimenType: EBUS-guided transbronchial needle biopsy cell block
cases.clinicalHistory: 62-year-old heavy smoker with bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy, hyponatremia, and a right hilar mass.

image.caption: Small cell carcinoma with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, and necrosis.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: radiologic findings of three mcc cases presenting as a soft tissue bone mass a axial t1 weighted and b axial t2 weighted fat saturated mri images of case 1 showing two lesions in the inguinal region arrow and gluteal region arrowhead with t1 isointensity to muscle a and t2 hyperintensity b c axial and d sagittal t2 weighted mri images of case 2 showing a slightly hypointense lesion in the second thoracic vertebra arrow in the transversal c and sagittal plane d and additional similar lesions in other thoracic vertebrae e f diagnostic imaging of case 3 showing a lesion on the upper arm e ultrasound image depicting a hypoechogenic partially ill defined mass f axial t1 weighted mri image showing a mass arrow with isointensity to muscle

diagnosticSteps: Recognize a high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma with small blue cells, nuclear molding, crush artifact, necrosis, and high mitotic activity.
Confirm epithelial and neuroendocrine differentiation while distinguishing from lymphoma and basaloid squamous carcinoma.
Correlate with imaging to support pulmonary origin and mediastinal nodal involvement.
Report diagnosis succinctly and preserve remaining material for clinically directed testing.

ancillaryOptions.name: Synaptophysin, chromogranin, and INSM1
ancillaryOptions.result: Neuroendocrine marker positivity supports small cell carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: Ki-67
ancillaryOptions.result: Very high proliferation supports high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD45
ancillaryOptions.result: Negative staining helps exclude lymphoma in a crushed small blue cell biopsy.

cases.reportingTarget: Mediastinal lymph node, EBUS-guided biopsy: Metastatic small cell carcinoma, compatible with pulmonary origin in the clinical setting.
pitfalls: Do not mistake crush artifact for lymphoma without epithelial and hematolymphoid markers.
Do not overinterpret focal TTF-1 because extrapulmonary small cell carcinomas may also express it.
Do not delay urgent diagnosis with unnecessary expanded panels.

cases.title: Pleural biopsy: malignant mesothelioma, epithelioid type
cases.clinicalHistory: 75-year-old patient with asbestos exposure history, unilateral pleural effusion, and diffuse nodular pleural thickening.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: a single cell rna sequencing scrna seq atlas of human parietal pleura a study pipeline samples obtained by video assisted thoracic surgery vats were disaggregated to single cells with collagenase viable cells were collected by fluorescence activated cell sorting and immediately processed by 10x genomics chromium controller b uniform manifold approximation and projection umap of 63 748 pleural cells from eight donors are colour coded by cell type c umap projection of canonical marker gene expression mesothelial cells intelectin 1 itln1 mesothelin msln and podoplanin pdpn fibroblasts collagen type i 2 chain col1a2 and platelet derived growth factor receptor pdgfrb pericytes regulator of g protein signaling 5 rgs5 smooth muscle cells smooth muscle actin 2 acta2 and myosin heavy chain 11 myh11 and endothelial cells platelet and endothelial cell adhesion molecule 1 pecam1 d heat map of the relative average expression of the most strongly enriched genes for each cluster presented as log fold change of one cluster versus all others grouped by cell type all gene expression values are normalised across rows e violin plots of dermatopontin dpt collagen type xv 1 chain col15a1 peptidase inhibitor 16 pi16 and microfibril associated protein 5 mfap5 expression f umap visualisation of 32 750 stromal cells eight populations identified through graph based clustering are indicated by colour and annotated as mesothelial pi16 col15a1 pi16 + col15a1 pi16 + col15a1 + pi16 col15a1 + pi16 + angptl7 + pericyte and smooth muscle g heat map of the relative average expression of the most strongly enriched genes for each cluster in f presented as log fold change of one cluster versus all others grouped by cell type all gene expression values are normalised across rows angptl7 angiopoietin related protein 7

diagnosticSteps: Establish invasive epithelioid mesothelial proliferation involving pleural tissue rather than reactive mesothelial hyperplasia.
Apply a balanced mesothelial and carcinoma immunopanel to support lineage.
Use BAP1 loss, MTAP loss, or CDKN2A deletion testing to support malignancy when invasion is limited or equivocal.
Avoid definitive subtype overreach if biopsy is too small to exclude biphasic disease.

ancillaryOptions.name: Calretinin, WT1, and D2-40
ancillaryOptions.result: Positive mesothelial markers support mesothelial lineage.

ancillaryOptions.name: Claudin-4 and MOC31
ancillaryOptions.result: Negative carcinoma markers help exclude metastatic carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: BAP1 and MTAP immunohistochemistry
ancillaryOptions.result: Loss supports malignant mesothelioma in the right morphologic context.

cases.reportingTarget: Pleura, thoracoscopic biopsy: Malignant mesothelioma, epithelioid type, supported by mesothelial immunophenotype and loss of BAP1 expression.
pitfalls: Do not diagnose mesothelioma from cytology alone without accepted ancillary or tissue support.
Do not use a one-marker panel to separate mesothelioma from carcinoma.
Do not ignore metastatic lung adenocarcinoma in pleural biopsies.

cases.title: Thymectomy: type B2 thymoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 54-year-old patient with myasthenia gravis and a 5.1 cm encapsulated anterior mediastinal mass.

image.caption: Thymoma with abundant lymphocytes and polygonal epithelial cells.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: comparison of postoperative radiotherapy port management under r0 resection among different guidelines the colored bubbles represent the patients in whom port may be recommended by guidelines nccn national comprehensive cancer network jlcs japan lung cancer society esmo european society for medical oncology a a program in evidence based care pebc was developed by ontario health cancer care ontario canada suggesting that port is recommended for tnm stage iii iv thymomas b thymic malignancies tyme was founded by the italian collaborative group c the china anti cancer association caca suggested that port be recommended for tnm stage ii iiia thymomas with type b2 b3 and all tnm stage iv thymomas

diagnosticSteps: Classify the epithelial neoplasm using WHO thymoma criteria, balancing epithelial cell density and lymphocyte-rich background.
Assess capsule, mediastinal fat, pleura, pericardium, and adjacent organ invasion.
Ink and evaluate margins, especially areas of capsular disruption or adherent fat.
Assign stage using local reporting convention and include nodal evaluation if nodes are submitted.

ancillaryOptions.name: Pancytokeratin and p40/p63
ancillaryOptions.result: Highlights thymic epithelial cells and architecture.

ancillaryOptions.name: TdT
ancillaryOptions.result: Labels immature T cells and supports thymic lymphoid background.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD5 and CD117
ancillaryOptions.result: Diffuse epithelial positivity would raise concern for thymic carcinoma.

cases.reportingTarget: Thymus, thymectomy: Thymoma, WHO type B2, 5.1 cm, microscopically transcapsular into perithymic fat, margins negative.
pitfalls: Do not confuse type B2 thymoma with thymic carcinoma without cytologic and immunophenotypic support.
Do not omit microscopic transcapsular invasion.
Do not stage from gross encapsulation alone.

cases.title: Apical lung wedge: ruptured bleb
cases.specimenType: Apical wedge resection for spontaneous pneumothorax
cases.clinicalHistory: 24-year-old tall thin patient with recurrent spontaneous pneumothorax and thoracoscopic apical bleb stapling.

image.caption: Apical subpleural bleb from wedge resection specimen.

diagnosticSteps: Confirm the specimen is an expected stapled apical lung wedge with subpleural bleb or bulla formation.
Review history for red flags such as malignancy, infection, interstitial lung disease, endometriosis, or diffuse cystic lung disease.
Assess grossly for nodules, consolidation, necrosis, or diffuse parenchymal abnormality that would require microscopic sampling.
Use gross-only sign-out only if local policy permits and the clinical and gross findings are concordant.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: No ancillary testing is required for an uncomplicated apical bleb specimen.

ancillaryOptions.name: AFB and GMS stains
ancillaryOptions.result: Reserve for granulomas, necrosis, or clinical infection concern if tissue is submitted microscopically.

ancillaryOptions.name: Elastic stain
ancillaryOptions.result: May document pleural or parenchymal architecture if microscopic review is needed.

cases.reportingTarget: Lung, apical wedge resection: Subpleural bleb, gross diagnosis only.
requiredReportElements: no mass or consolidation if applicable
pitfalls: Do not sign out gross-only if imaging shows a nodule or diffuse cystic lung disease.
Do not ignore catamenial pneumothorax history, which may require sampling for thoracic endometriosis.
Do not omit staple line and wedge dimensions in the gross-only checklist.
